response:
THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY INTEL CO-FOUNDER GORDON E. MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR WILL DOUBLE ABOUT EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS OBSERVATION HAS DRIVEN THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING POWER AND TECHNOLOGY FOR DECADES. HOWEVER, AS WE APPROACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON-BASED TECHNOLOGY, IT BECOMES CLEAR THAT THE LAW MAY NO LONGER HOLD IN ITS ORIGINAL FORM. SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS ARE NOW EXPLORING ALTERNATIVE MATERIALS AND NEW COMPUTING PARADIGMS TO CONTINUE THE INNOVATION THAT THE LAW HAS HELPED TO SPUR.

***  
THE EXACT POINT AT WHICH THE LAW OF MOORE WILL CEASE TO BE VALID IS DIFFICULT TO PREDICT, AS IT DEPENDS ON ADVANCES IN MATERIAL SCIENCE, MANUFACTURING TECHNIQUES, AND COMPUTING ARCHITECTURES. WHILE SOME BELIEVE THE LAW MAY STILL APPLY IN A MODIFIED FORM FOR DECADES TO COME, OTHERS ARGUE THAT THE END OF MOORE'S LAW IS NOW NEAR. REGARDLESS OF THE OUTCOME, THE IMPACT OF THIS SHIFT WILL BE PROFOUND, AFFECTING EVERY ASPECT OF MODERN TECHNOLOGY AND SOCIETY. DO NOT
